---
title: "Internal Triggers"
format: html
---
# Internal Triggers

Internal triggers include thoughts, emotions, body sensations, and memories that increase urge to drink. Common examples are stress, anger, loneliness, shame, overconfidence ("I can handle just one"), physical discomfort, and mental replay of past drinking relief. Internal triggers are especially important because they can show up anywhere, even in safe environments.

A key skill in recovery is learning to observe inner experiences without automatically acting on them. Urges usually rise and fall like waves. When you label what is happening ("this is anxiety," "this is craving," "this is an old thought"), you create space between feeling and behavior.

In many recovery approaches that focus on thoughts and behaviors, internal triggers are treated as early warning signals rather than commands. The more quickly you notice and name an internal cue, the more options you have to choose a response that protects sobriety.

## Noticing Practice
For the next week, complete this once daily or when urge appears.

| What I noticed inside me | Intensity (0-10) | What I did instead of drinking | Result after 20 minutes |
|---|---:|---|---|
|  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |

## Reflection
Which internal trigger shows up most often for you, and what response lowers it fastest?
